question.label: Week three check-in
question.name: What's been most on your mind at work this week, Sam?
question.description: Safe opener that lets Sam set context before any friction is introduced.

answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.
note: He can execute tasks but is second-guessing whether his approach matches team expectations, signaling mild strain and a clear expectations gap.

question.label: Team expectations
question.name: When you second-guess, what team expectation feels hardest to read?
question.description: Drills into the specific expectation gap behind his second-guessing.

answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.
note: He names a specific expectation mismatch between design and API details that is still costing him time, signaling a mild clarity gap.

question.label: Design API mismatch
question.name: When design and API details clash, what usually ends up unclear to you?
question.description: Drills into the specific mismatch he mentioned so the manager can remove that expectation gap.

answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.
note: He names a specific approval-path gap before shipping, which is a strong clarity problem in current workflow expectations.

question.label: Thread follow
question.name: always sure should — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
question.description: Runtime thread-follow injected because planner did not follow substantive answer.

answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.
note: He delays asking for help to solve things himself first, which strongly signals an unclear escalation/approval path; [THREAD-DEFERRED] self-solving before asking could be picked up next session.

question.label: Shipping hesitation
question.name: What usually makes you pause before shipping?
question.description: Distinguishes confidence, tooling, and process reasons behind late reassurance-seeking.

answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.
note: Names a specific missing condition — fast, specific feedback — implying current hesitation is driven by low confidence and unclear expectations around shipping.

question.label: Fast feedback
question.name: What kind of feedback helps you move quicker here?
question.description: Clarifies the exact feedback Sam needs so shipping hesitation can be reduced.

answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.
note: He names a specific clarity gap around differing quality bars for quick fixes versus core flows, which is also mildly slowing his momentum.

question.label: Quality bars gap
question.name: Where do the quality bars feel most different to you right now?
question.description: Drills into the specific quality-bar gap so the manager can remove ambiguity around shipping expectations.

answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.
note: Names a specific missing support mechanism — regular pairing to avoid getting stuck silently — which is a strong current clarity gap. [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] pairing once or twice a week as support need.

question.label: Independence support
question.name: What support from me would help you ship with less second-guessing?
question.description: Surfaces the manager touchpoint that would reduce reassurance dependence over the next two weeks.

answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.
note: Names a specific missing support — defining what good looks like on the next two tickets — which signals current clarity is lacking and confidence to act is dampened.

question.label: Next focus
question.name: Given what we covered, where do you want to focus first?
question.description: Closes on a concrete next step and lets Sam choose the most useful immediate priority.

answer: I want to start by pairing on my next ticket so I can see the approval bar in practice.
note: He named a concrete first step—pairing on the next ticket to learn the approval bar in practice—which signals improved clarity and a learning-oriented next move.
